description: Enumerations for relationship taxonomy, dynamic type, and power balance between characters.

relationship_type.description: The fundamental social category of the relationship between two characters.

values.definition: A biological, adoptive, or functional parent-child relationship.
values.example_usage: Parent and adult child; guardian and ward.

values.definition: A biological, adoptive, or functional sibling relationship.
values.example_usage: Brothers, sisters, half-siblings, stepsiblings.

values.definition: A relationship between extended family members (aunts, uncles, cousins, grandparents, in-laws).
values.example_usage: Grandparent and grandchild; cousins; in-law relationships.

values.definition: A non-biological relationship functioning with the emotional force of family.
values.example_usage: Chosen family; found family; deep long-term bonds that replace or supplement biological family.

values.definition: A current or recent romantic partnership.
values.example_usage: Spouse, partner, boyfriend/girlfriend, lover.

values.definition: A previous romantic relationship that has ended but remains relevant.
values.example_usage: Ex-spouse, ex-partner; former lover.

values.definition: A romantic interest that is not reciprocated.
values.example_usage: Unrequited love; one-sided crush; secret longing.

values.definition: A non-romantic relationship based on mutual regard, history, and care.
values.example_usage: Best friends, old friends, casual friends.

values.definition: Colleagues or equals within a shared professional context.
values.example_usage: Co-workers, fellow academics, bandmates.

values.definition: A professional relationship with a clear power differential (boss/employee, teacher/student, mentor/mentee).
values.example_usage: Supervisor and subordinate; professor and student.

values.definition: A relationship defined by competition for a shared goal, resource, or recognition.
values.example_usage: Competing characters with parallel goals.

values.definition: The primary oppositional relationship of the story.
values.example_usage: The antagonist and the protagonist in direct opposition.

values.definition: Active hostility; the characters are in conflict and wish each other harm.
values.example_usage: Sworn enemies; characters with antagonistic intent.

values.definition: A relationship of mutual utility that may or may not involve personal connection.
values.example_usage: Political allies; strategic partners; convenient collaborators.

values.definition: A relationship of minimal depth; the characters know of each other without significant history.
values.example_usage: Neighbors, familiar strangers, people who have met once.

values.definition: No prior relationship; the characters meet for the first time in the scene context.
values.example_usage: A chance encounter; first meeting.

values.definition: A relationship defined by the characters' roles within a shared institution, with little personal history.
values.example_usage: Doctor/patient, guard/prisoner, priest/parishioner.

values.definition: The relationship type is deliberately unclear or contested by the characters themselves.
values.example_usage: A relationship that doesn't fit categories; one in active transition.

relationship_dynamic.description: The dominant behavioral or emotional pattern governing the relationship in the current story moment.

values.definition: The relationship is characterized by mutual or one-directional care, validation, and support.
values.example_usage: A friend who helps without agenda; a mentor's positive guidance.

values.definition: The relationship is characterized by opposition, conflict, or active competing interests.
values.example_usage: A rivalry in active combat; an ongoing dispute.

values.definition: One character is significantly reliant on the other for physical, emotional, or material well-being.
values.example_usage: A caretaker relationship; one who cannot function without the other.

values.definition: One character is using the other through deception, emotional leverage, or indirect psychological pressure.
values.example_usage: An abusive relationship; a character being groomed.

values.definition: One character acts primarily to shield the other from harm.
values.example_usage: A guardian protecting a ward; a sibling who takes on harm to protect another.

values.definition: Both characters are vying for the same goal, resource, or recognition.
values.example_usage: Rivals in open competition; colleagues competing for a promotion.

values.definition: The relationship is characterized by conflicting feelings — the characters both value and resent each other.
values.example_usage: Toxic love; necessary enemies; frenemies.

values.definition: The relationship maintains surface form without authentic connection; emotional distance is the defining quality.
values.example_usage: An estranged family relationship; a dead marriage maintained for social appearances.

values.definition: The relationship is characterized by emotional closeness, mutual disclosure, and genuine understanding.
values.example_usage: Deep friendship; loving marriage; trusted confidant.

values.definition: The relationship is purely functional — mutual service without personal investment.
values.example_usage: Hired help; professional relationships without warmth; alliances of convenience.

values.definition: The relationship is defined primarily by power and rank, with the power differential actively shaping all interactions.
values.example_usage: Boss and subordinate; ruler and subject; master and apprentice with active inequality.

values.definition: The relationship dynamic is in active transition — what it is now is not what it was or will be.
values.example_usage: A relationship in crisis or evolution; a friendship becoming something else.

power_balance.description: The distribution of power (social, institutional, emotional, physical, informational) between the two characters in this relationship at this point in the story.

values.definition: Character A holds significantly more power than Character B in this relationship at this point.
values.example_usage: The employer in a relationship with their employee; the parent of a young child.

values.definition: Character B holds significantly more power than Character A in this relationship at this point.
values.example_usage: Inverse of the above — depends on which character is defined as A and B.

values.definition: Power is approximately balanced; neither character has a decisive or consistent advantage.
values.example_usage: Peers; mutual friends; equals in an alliance.

values.definition: Control of power is actively disputed — the relationship itself is partly about who holds power.
values.example_usage: A power struggle; a shifting hierarchy; rivals fighting for dominance.

values.definition: Power shifts depending on domain — one character holds physical power, the other holds informational power, etc.
values.example_usage: A jailer who holds physical control but whom a prisoner has blackmail material against.
